Not freshers or call centres consult India’s top 5% insurance experts, powered with sophisticated research on Beshak. Book your FREE call NOW!

Choosing health insurance today feels exactly like opening a giant restaurant menu. Everything looks fancy. Everything claims to be the chef’s special. 
But unless someone explains what is actually worth ordering you can walk out paying a huge bill without getting the meal you needed.

ICICI Lombard Elevate is one of those dishes that looks premium and unlimited at first glance. But what does it really offer once you taste it We broke it down the way a friend would if you were choosing from a confusing menu.

Key Highlights:
✅ Infinite Sum Insured and how practical it really is
✅ One-time unlimited claim and who actually needs it
✅ Infinite reset that refills your cover multiple times
✅ Infinite loyalty bonus that grows your cover every year
✅ Worldwide cover and whether it is worth the extra cost

If you have ever stared at a policy brochure and felt lost this video will feel like someone finally sitting beside you and telling you what is worth ordering and what you can skip.

comments.text: Chat with a Beshak Expert Now: wa.link/beshak
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: Sir u forgot opd plus option on icici elevate
comments.author: @2.mirahmeduddinalikhan158

comments.text:
WE WILL HAVE TO GIVE UNDERWRITNG FOR EVERY RENEWAL ABOUT OUR HEALTH CONDITIONS AND ICICI CAN REJECT RENEWAL  OR CAN INCREASE PREMIUM OR REJECT A CLAIM ON THE BASIS OF THIS. THIS IS THE BIGGEST DRAW BACK. NO OTHER COMPANY ASKS FOR AN UNDERWRITING
THE RESTORATION BENEFIT IS NOT ACTIVATED IN FIRST CLAIM. THEN WHAT IS THE USE OF IT
comments.author: @vimarsakan1

comments.text:
Hi, we understand why this feels worrying but a small clarification may help here. In retail health insurance, insurers cannot arbitrarily reject renewals or cancel a policy just because health conditions change. ICICI Lombard also issued a clarification regarding this recently. Renewal is guaranteed for life, except in cases of proven fraud or misrepresentation. Asking for health information at renewal does not automatically mean fresh underwriting or rejection, though premiums can change as per product rules approved by IRDAI. Claim rejection also cannot be done just because of a condition if it is otherwise covered under the policy terms. On restoration, you’re right that it does not apply to the first claim of the year. Its purpose is to act as a backup for subsequent hospitalisations in the same policy year, not as a replacement for the base sum insured. That’s why restoration should be seen as an additional safety net, not the main cover.
These concerns are valid and this is exactly why understanding renewal rules and benefit fine print upfront is important.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text:
Great information. 

Additionally, please include an Industry Average section for all points, but only if that makes sense—it could add another layer of perspective through a more convex lens.
comments.author: @hardiksumariya09

comments.text: Thanks for your feedback! We’ll discuss this internally and see how we can include this perspective in our website insights and upcoming content.

comments.text: Non medical expenses add on only pays till base sum insured amount not from cumulative bonus not from super unlimited bonus which is a big down 👎🏼, if you have a serious or longer hospitalisation it is going to cost you kindly mention this as well. & Like ditto push their partnered insurance beshak prioritises icici i had 3 consultation & everytime they pushed icici & denied any other company i mentioned & advised not to be so picky in conditions. Irony 😅
comments.author: @ujjawalchoubey8792

comments.text:
Hi, thanks for pointing this out. In some plans (including Elevate), non-medical coverage and other benefits are payable only up to the base sum insured. But if that feels like a drawback, it often means the base cover itself may not be adequate. We recommend not treating bonus or restoration as part of base cover. Such perks are meant to be buffers, not something to rely on for primary expenses. The base sum insured should be strong enough on its own to handle major hospitalisations. We’ve explained this in detail here:
https://www.beshak.org/insurance/health-insurance/articles/do-not-consider-ncb-and-restoration-as-core-part-of-your-cover/
Regarding second part, thank you for flagging this. We’re genuinely sorry for the experience where you felt pushed towards a particular insurer or plan. At Beshak, we have strict internal compliance guidelines to ensure our advisors remain unbiased and help customers make fully informed, independent decisions - not steer them towards any specific company. As discussed with one of our community members, we are reviewing your case & our priority is always transparency and customer-first advice.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text:
Sir could please elaborate what type of daycare treatment is allowed 
Is there a list like aditya birla and careinsurance  which show 586 day care treatments covered
Is there a master list as laid by irdai for categorizing Day care treatment
comments.author: @arunmohanty123

comments.text:
Hi there! Thanks for your question. A day-care treatment refers to any medical or surgical procedure that’s performed under general or local anaesthesia in a hospital or day-care centre in less than 24 hours because of technological advancement but would have otherwise required more than 24 hours of hospitalisation.

IRDAI doesn’t publish or mandate a master list of such procedures. Each insurer defines this differently - some insurers cover a specific list of day-care treatments, while others cover all that meet the definition. It’s always best to check your policy wording to see which approach your insurer follows. In the case of ICICI Lombard Elevate, all day-care procedures that meet the definition are covered.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text:
Hi there ! Thanks a lot for doing this ! Would appreciate if you could help me with the following questions :

1.) Over time, an insurer might shut down a policy and migrate its existing policy holders over to a new policy. In such cases, exactly what HAS TO BE (i.e., not optional for the insurer) carried over apart from the waiting periods served and the sum insured ? What all are optional ? For example, is the bonus carried over ? How about the option of an aggregate deductible in the old policy ? Can the aggregate deductible versus discount percentage matrix change in the new policy ? In the specific case of HDFC Optima Secure, their policy documents say that along with sum insured, the bonus is also carried forward. However, they do not explicitly call the 2x/3x benefit as bonus anywhere in their policy. Does that mean those 2x/3x benefits expire if the policy is migrated by HDFC to a new policy ?

2.a) Can new features be added to an existing policy ? For example, HDFC Optima Secure currently offers a max sum insured of 2 CR. Does the IRDAI allow them to increase this upper bound over time ? Even if such an option is allowed, can a policyholder who opted for the policy before such an increase in the upper bound opt for an increase in sum insured beyond the previous limit ? Just trying to understand whether someone who opts for a 2 CR policy today is ever able to go beyond that in the future, say 30, 40 years from now.

2.b) What features in a policy can change over time ? For example, as of now, HDFC Optima Secure offers a discount of 40% on the premium of a 20L policy if a deductible of 50k is chosen. Can this change in the future ?

3.) Does fresh medical underwriting happen at the time an add-on is chosen ? For example, if somebody takes a policy today and opts for an add-on 10 years from now, will he have to report his health status then ?

4.) Does a policyholder have the right to lifetime renewability for an add-on just like the base policy ? Or can the insurer shut down the add-on any time without leaving any alternative for the policyholder ?

5.) Is it possible to change the intermediary for a policy ? This is because over long time-frames, say 40 to 50 years, many things could change - let's say the agent is not around any more or no longer in the business or perhaps not quite up to the mark at his job.
comments.author: @Arka-g7q
